metadata.title: An extremely active repeating fast radio burst source in a likely non-magneto-ionic environment
metadata.abstract: Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among _∼_ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr _[−]_[1] , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was _−_ 0.4 _±_ 0.3 rad m _[−]_[2] with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.
metadata.introduction:
Corresponding author: Yi Feng, D. Li, Y.-K. Zhang, Chao-Wei Tsai yifeng@zhejianglab.com, dili@nao.cas.cn, ykzhang@nao.cas.cn, cwtsai@nao.cas.cn 

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio transients first discovered by Lorimer et al. (2007). While their cosmological origin and energetic nature make them ideal tools for probing cosmic web, Galactic haloes, baryons, etc. (Ravi et al. 2016, Prochaska et al. 2019, Macquart et al. 2020), their progenitors and radiation mechanisms are still unknown. A particularly interesting subset of FRBs is the so-called repeating FRBs, which recurrently 

Feng et al. 

2 

emit radio bursts. Among _∼_ 800 FRBs, _∼_ 60 are repeating FRBs[1] (Xu et al. 2023). 

High levels of activity from a repeating FRB source discovered by Canadian Hydrogen Intensity Mapping Experiment (CHIME), FRB 20220912A, was announced in October 2022 (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022). McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration (2022) reported nine bursts from FRB 20220912A in three days of observations and estimated a burst rate as high as 200-300 bursts per day at _∼_ Jy ms fluence thresholds. FRB 20220912A has a dispersion measure (DM) of 219.46 pc cm _[−]_[3] (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022). The maximum Galactic DM contribution along this line of sight is about 125 and 122 pc cm _[−]_[3] for NE2001 model (Cordes & Lazio 2002) and YMW16 model (Yao et al. 2017) , respectively. This active repeating FRB was then followed up by telescopes worldwide, including the Deep Synoptic Array (DSA-110) (Ravi 2022), the Big Scanning Antenna (BSA) (Fedorova & Rodin 2022), the Stockert telescope (Herrmann 2022), the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) (Feng et al. 2022b), the Effelsberg (Kirsten et al. 2022), the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope (FAST) (Zhang et al. 2022a), the Arecibo 12-m (Perera et al. 2022), the Allen Telescope Array (Sheikh et al. 2022), the Tianlai (Yu et al. 2022), the Nancay radio telescope (NRT) (Hewitt et al. 2023), and the upgraded Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT) (Bhusare et al. 2022). DSA-110 localized FRB 20220912A to Right Ascension (RA) 23 _[h]_ 09 _[m]_ 04.9 _[s]_ , Declination (Dec) +48 _[◦]_ 42 _[′]_ 25.4 _[′′]_ (J2000 equinox), consistent with the galaxy PSO J347.2702+48.7066 (Ravi et al. 2023) at a redshift of 0.0771. 

High levels of activity has also been observed in other repeating FRB sources. For example, FRB 20121102A and FRB 20201124A had peak burst rate of 122 hr _[−]_[1] (Li et al. 2021) and 542 hr _[−]_[1] (Zhang et al. 2022b), respectively. Both rate measurements were obtained by the FAST (Nan et al. 2011, Li et al. 2018). The burst rate of the overall repeater population typically ranges from 0.1 hr _[−]_[1] to _∼_ 10 hr _[−]_[1] (CHIME/FRB Collaboration et al. 2019, Fonseca et al. 2020, Luo et al. 2020, Chime/Frb Collaboration et al. 2023, Kumar et al. 2023). We caution that the burst rate of some repeaters could be higher because a fraction of weak bursts cannot be detected. FRB 20121102A and FRB 20201124A are likely located in complex environments. FRB 20121102A has a large rotation measure (RM) scatter ( _σ_ RM) of 30 _._ 9 rad _/_ m[2] (Feng et al. 2022c), which 

has been attributed to multi-path scattering arising from complex environment. The RM of FRB 20121102A decreased from 1 _._ 03 _×_ 10[5] rad m _[−]_[2] to 6 _._ 7 _×_ 10[4] rad m _[−]_[2] between January 2017 and August 2019 (Michilli et al. 2018, Hilmarsson et al. 2021). The RM decreased to 3 _._ 1 _×_ 10[4] rad m _[−]_[2] in 2023 (Feng et al. 2023), continuing the decreasing trend. The large RM scatter and variable RMs of FRB 20121102A indicate that there are some complex dynamic magneto-ionic environments near the FRB source, which may be related to supernova remnants (SNR) or wind nebulae (Piro & Gaensler 2018, Margalit & Metzger 2018, Zhao & Wang 2021, Katz 2022, Yang et al. 2022, Yang et al. 2023). FRB 20201124A shows irregular short-time variation of the RM followed by a constant RM (Xu et al. 2022), suggesting a complicated, dynamically evolving, magnetized immediate environment. A similar behavior (irregular RM variation followed by a constant RM) has been observed in pulsar binary systems, which implies some FRB source might be in a binary system with an elliptical orbit (Wang et al. 2022a, Zhao et al. 2023, Yang et al. 2023). 

Unlike FRB 20121102A and FRB 20201124A, here we report the likely non-magneto-ionic environment of the active repeating FRB 20220912A. Our observations and data processing procedures are described in Section 2. Our results are presented in Section 3. We discuss the results and conclude in Section 4.

evidence.original_text: Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among ∼ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was − 0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio transients first discovered by Lorimer et al. (2007). While their cosmological origin and energetic nature make them ideal tools for probing cosmic web, Galactic haloes, baryons, etc. (Ravi et al. 2016, Prochaska et al. 2019, Macquart et al. 2020), their progenitors and radiation mechanisms are still unknown. A particularly interesting subset of FRBs is the so-called repeating FRBs, which recurrently emit radio bursts. Among ∼ 800 FRBs, ∼ 60 are repeating FRBs (Xu et al. 2023). High levels of activity from a repeating FRB source discovered by Canadian Hydrogen Intensity Mapping Experiment (CHIME), FRB 20220912A, was announced in October 2022 (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022). McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration (2022) reported nine bursts from FRB 20220912A in three days of observations and estimated a burst rate as high as 200-300 bursts per day at ∼ Jy ms fluence thresholds. FRB 20220912A has a dispersion measure (DM) of 219.46 pc cm −3 (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022). The maximum Galactic DM contribution along this line of sight is about 125 and 122 pc cm −3 for NE2001 model (Cordes & Lazio 2002) and YMW16 model (Yao et al. 2017) , respectively.

Active repeating FRBs 20121102A and 20201124A show signs of being in complex environments. Although FRB 20220912A is extremely active, the environment of FRB 20220912A seem to be non-magneto-ionic. We show the RM of FRB 20220912A in Figure 1. The RMs have been corrected for the effect of the ionosphere. We calculate the ionosphere’s RM by using a python code - `IonFR` (Sotomayor-Beltran et al. 2013) and global ionospheric map (GIM) products. The GIM products are available on NASA’s website in the IONosphere EXchange (IONEX) format. For our GBT observation, the ionosphere contribution is 1 .69 ± 0 .06 rad m −2 . In Figure 1, we only selected bursts with RM error bars smaller than 5 rad m −2 . We note that burst 89 has a RM of 14.5 ± 0.6 rad m −2 . This RM measurement is an outlier and is caused by frequency-dependant polarization. Excluding this measurement and bursts with RM error bars larger than 5 rad m −2 , our GBT measurements have a mean RM of − 0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 . We did not observe obvious intraday RM change in FRB 20220912A in contrast to other active repeating FRBs. FRB 20121102A shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m −2 (Hilmarsson et al. 2021). Similar to FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B also shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m −2 (AnnaThomas et al. 2023). FRB 20201124A has a smaller intraday RM change of a few tens rad m −2 (Xu et al. 2022).

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among ∼ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was − 0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

Out of the 128 detected bursts, 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. For bursts with peak flux density greater than 500 mJy, 56% of the bursts have circular polarization with signal-to-noise ratio > 5. The highest absolute fractional circular polarization reaches 58%. FRB 20220912A is the 4th reported repeating FRB with circular polarization. The previous reported repeating FRBs with circular polarization are all active FRBs, namely FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022), FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a). The fraction of bursts with circular polarization is less than 10% for FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a), much smaller than FRB 20220912A. Polarization angle swings are observed in some bursts of FRB 20220912A. The other repeating FRBs with polarization angle swings include FRB 20180301A (Luo et al. 2020) and FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022). In Figure 2, we show polarization angles, pulse profiles and dynamic spectra of a few representative bursts. As shown in the figure, bursts 2 and 89 have clear polarization angle swings. We also note that burst 52 shows downward drift in frequency (Hessels et al. 2019).

High levels of activity from a repeating FRB source discovered by Canadian Hydrogen Intensity Mapping Experiment (CHIME), FRB 20220912A, was announced in October 2022 (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022). McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration (2022) reported nine bursts from FRB 20220912A in three days of observations and estimated a burst rate as high as 200-300 bursts per day at ∼ Jy ms fluence thresholds. FRB 20220912A has a dispersion measure (DM) of 219.46 pc cm −3 (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022). The maximum Galactic DM contribution along this line of sight is about 125 and 122 pc cm −3 for NE2001 model (Cordes & Lazio 2002) and YMW16 model (Yao et al. 2017) , respectively. This active repeating FRB was then followed up by telescopes worldwide, including the Deep Synoptic Array (DSA-110) (Ravi 2022), the Big Scanning Antenna (BSA) (Fedorova & Rodin 2022), the Stockert telescope (Herrmann 2022), the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) (Feng et al. 2022b), the Effelsberg (Kirsten et al. 2022), the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope (FAST) (Zhang et al. 2022a), the Arecibo 12-m (Perera et al. 2022), the Allen Telescope Array (Sheikh et al. 2022), the Tianlai (Yu et al. 2022), the Nancay radio telescope (NRT) (Hewitt et al. 2023), and the upgraded Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT) (Bhusare et al. 2022). DSA-110 localized FRB 20220912A to Right Ascension (RA) 23 h 09 m 04.9 s , Declination (Dec) +48 ◦ 42 ′ 25.4 ′′ (J2000 equinox), consistent with the galaxy PSO J347.2702+48.7066 (Ravi et al. 2023) at a redshift of 0.0771.

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among ∼ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was − 0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among ∼ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was − 0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among ∼ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was − 0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

Out of the 128 detected bursts, 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. For bursts with peak flux density greater than 500 mJy, 56% of the bursts have circular polarization with signal-to-noise ratio > 5. The highest absolute fractional circular polarization reaches 58%. FRB 20220912A is the 4th reported repeating FRB with circular polarization. The previous reported repeating FRBs with circular polarization are all active FRBs, namely FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022), FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a). The fraction of bursts with circular polarization is less than 10% for FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a), much smaller than FRB 20220912A. Polarization angle swings are observed in some bursts of FRB 20220912A. The other repeating FRBs with polarization angle swings include FRB 20180301A (Luo et al. 2020) and FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022). In Figure 2, we show polarization angles, pulse profiles and dynamic spectra of a few representative bursts. As shown in the figure, bursts 2 and 89 have clear polarization angle swings. We also note that burst 52 shows downward drift in frequency (Hessels et al. 2019).

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among ∼ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was − 0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

We detected 128 bursts from FRB 20220912A in 1.4 hours with the GBT (see Methods 2.1 for details of the observation). The time of arrivals, peak flux density, width, fluence, energy, DM, RM and degrees of linear and circular polarization of the bursts are listed in Table 1. The average burst rate is about 90 hr⁻¹, which is the highest for fast radio burst observations at the GBT or any other (sub)-100 meter radio telescopes. The previous highest burst rate at the GBT is 21 hr⁻¹ of FRB 20121102A (Gajjar et al. 2018). Nimmo et al. (2023) reported a burst storm of 53 bursts occuring within only 40 minutes from FRB 20200120E at Effelsberg telescope, which corresponds to a burst rate of 80 hr⁻¹. Active repeating FRBs 20121102A and 20201124A show signs of being in complex environments. Although FRB 20220912A is extremely active, the environment of FRB 20220912A seem to be non-magneto-ionic. We show the RM of FRB 20220912A in Figure 1. The RMs have been corrected for the effect of the ionosphere. We calculate the ionosphere’s RM by using a python code - `IonFR` (Sotomayor-Beltran et al. 2013) and global ionospheric map (GIM) products. The GIM products are available on NASA’s website in the IONosphere EXchange (IONEX) format. For our GBT observation, the ionosphere contribution is 1.69 ± 0.06 rad m⁻². In Figure 1, we only selected bursts with RM error bars smaller than 5 rad m⁻². We note that burst 89 has a RM of 14.5 ± 0.6 rad m⁻². This RM measurement is an outlier and is caused by frequency-dependant polarization. Excluding this measurement and bursts with RM error bars larger than 5 rad m⁻², our GBT measurements have a mean RM of −0.4 ± 0.3 rad m⁻². We did not observe obvious intraday RM change in FRB 20220912A in contrast to other active repeating FRBs. FRB 20121102A shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m⁻² (Hilmarsson et al. 2021). Similar to FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B also shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m⁻² (AnnaThomas et al. 2023). FRB 20201124A has a smaller intraday RM change of a few tens rad m⁻² (Xu et al. 2022). In Figure 1, we also show the RM measurements from CHIME (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022) and NRT (Hewitt et al. 2023). The RM does not change in about a month’s time. The estimated Galactic RM contribution in this line of sight is −15 ± 11 rad m⁻² (Hutschenreuter et al. 2022). The local environment of FRB 20220912A thus contributes little RM. The small and stable RMs indicate a non-magnetoionic environment of FRB 20220912A. The observation of FRB 20201124A implies that the RM variation could be intermittent for a certain source, meanwhile, the small mean value might be due to the cancellation of RM at different region. Thus, in the future we will conduct a long-term monitoring campaign for FRB 20220912A, which would reveal whether such a source indeed has a non-magneto-ionic environment.

Out of the 128 detected bursts, 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. For bursts with peak flux density greater than 500 mJy, 56% of the bursts have circular polarization with signal-to-noise ratio > 5. The highest absolute fractional circular polarization reaches 58%. FRB 20220912A is the 4th reported repeating FRB with circular polarization. The previous reported repeating FRBs with circular polarization are all active FRBs, namely FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022), FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a). The fraction of bursts with circular polarization is less than 10% for FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a), much smaller than FRB 20220912A. Polarization angle swings are observed in some bursts of FRB 20220912A. The other repeating FRBs with polarization angle swings include FRB 20180301A (Luo et al. 2020) and FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022). In Figure 2, we show polarization angles, pulse profiles and dynamic spectra of a few representative bursts. As shown in the figure, bursts 2 and 89 have clear polarization angle swings. We also note that burst 52 shows downward drift in frequency (Hessels et al. 2019).

FRB 20220912A was observed between 1.1–1.9 GHz with the GBT’s L-Band receiver and the Versatile Green Bank Astronomical Spectrometer (VEGAS) digital backend (Prestage et al. 2015) on 24 October 2022 starting at 00:30:46 UTC and lasting 2 hours. After setting up and calibration, the on-source time was 1.4 hours. The data were coherently dedispersed at a DM of 219.46 pc cm⁻³ and recorded in the psrfits standard format (Hotan et al. 2004). Full-Stokes spectra were recorded every 81.92 µs with 0.195 MHz-wide channels.

entries.description: Data recorded in psrfits standard format with full-Stokes spectra

evidence.original_text: Data were calibrated using the psrchive package Hotan et al. (2004). On and off-source observations of the standard calibrator 3C 380 were used to measure the intensity of the L-Band receiver’s built-in noise diode. The noise diode was observed again at the position of FRB 20220912A prior to the main observations, and these data were used to flux calibrate each burst from the FRB. Polarization calibration was achieved by correcting for the differential gain and phase between the receptors through separate measurements of a noise diode signal injected at an angle of 45° from the linear receptors with the single-axis model using the PSRCHIVE software package. To excise radio frequency interference (RFI), we used the PSRCHIVE software package to adopt median filter to each burst in the frequency domain with the command paz and we also mitigated RFI of each burst manually with the command pazi.

entries.description: Polarization calibration using noise diode and RFI excision procedures

evidence.original_text: We measured the RM using the methods of Stokes QU-fitting (O’Sullivan et al. 2012). We selected positions where the signal exceeds the noise by 3 sigma for RM fitting. For each burst, we fitted an average RM. We calculated the degrees of linear polarization and circular polarization for bursts with RM detection. We first derotated the linear polarization with the measured RM. The measured linear polarization is overestimated in the presence of noise. Therefore we use the frequencyaveraged, de-biased total linear polarization (Everett & Weisberg 2001; Day et al. 2020): where σI is the Stokes I off-pulse standard deviation and Li is the measured frequency-averaged linear polarization of time sample i. We defined the degree of linear polarization as (ΣiL de-bias,i)/(ΣiIi) and that of circular polarization as (ΣiVi)/(ΣiIi), where the summation is over the time samples in one burst and Vi is the measured frequency-averaged circular polarization of time sample i. Defining I = ΣiIi, L = ΣiL de-bias,i and V = ΣiVi, uncertainties on the linear polarization fraction and circular polarization fraction are calculated as: where N is the number of time samples of the burst (signal exceeds the noise by 3σ), and ρ = L, V for linear and circular polarization fraction, respectively.

entries.description: RM measurement using Stokes QU-fitting and de-biased polarization fraction calculation

evidence.original_text: We use DM ~~p~~ hase for DM optimization. After the flux calibration, we calculated the peak flux density S peak and fluence F from the data. Due to the presence of RFI in many bursts, we determined the upper and lower cutoff frequencies (bandwidth) where the signal is submerged in noise, through visual inspection. The choice of bandwidth does not affect the energy calculation as long as it adequately covers the burst. In the case of bursts with substructure, we defined the burst bandwidth based on the widest structure within it. The pulse profile was obtained by averaging the flux in burst bandwidth along the frequency. The peak flux is the maximum value of the pulse profile with the time resolution of 655.36 µs. Specific fluence is the integration of the burst profile with respect to time, and the equivalent width W eq is computed by dividing the specific fluence by the peak flux. We calculate the isotropic equivalent burst energy following the Equation: where F = S peak × W eq is the specific fluence, ∆ν is the burst bandwidth. The adoption of ∆ν is more relevant for narrow-band FRBs, which is typically the case for the bursts in repeaters. DL = 360.86 Mpc is the luminosity distance of FRB 20220912A corresponding to the redshift z = 0.0771 (Ravi et al. 2023) adopting the standard Planck cosmological model (Planck Collaboration et al. 2016).

entries.description: DM optimization, flux/fluence/energy calculation with RFI-aware bandwidth selection

evidence.original_text: Pulse searching is grounded in our developed deep learning-based search tool (Zhang et al, in prep), named 2 DRAFTS . Here, we employ a classification model based on ResNet50V2 (He et al. 2016a,b) from the DRAFTS framework, which has been well-trained on real FRB data detected by FAST. The model receives time-frequency data after dispersion correction, with an input size of 1 × 512 × 512, and outputs the probability (ranging from 0 to 1) of an FRB being present in the data. For the GBT data, we apply dispersion compensation with DM = 219.46 pc cm⁻³, followed by fourfold data subsampling. The data are then divided into distinct time segments, each consisting of 4096 samples (post-subsampling), and reshaped into a size of 512 × 512. These segments are subsequently fed into the model for prediction. When the probability of a signal in the model-predicted data segment exceeds 50% (as the output of the softmax activation function > 0.5), we retain this data segment as a signal. This criterion differs from the traditional signal-to-noise ratio definition. Ultimately, 128 bursts are preserved.

entries.description: Burst detection using deep learning-based tool with specific probability threshold

evidence.original_text: Pulse searching is grounded in our developed deep learning-based search tool (Zhang et al, in prep), named 2 DRAFTS . Here, we employ a classification model based on ResNet50V2 (He et al. 2016a,b) from the DRAFTS framework, which has been well-trained on real FRB data detected by FAST. The model receives time-frequency data after dispersion correction, with an input size of 1 × 512 × 512, and outputs the probability (ranging from 0 to 1) of an FRB being present in the data. For the GBT data, we apply dispersion compensation with DM = 219.46 pc cm −3 , followed by fourfold data subsampling. The data are then divided into distinct time segments, each consisting of 4096 samples (post-subsampling), and reshaped into a size of 512 × 512. These segments are subsequently fed into the model for prediction. When the probability of a signal in the model-predicted data segment exceeds 50% (as the output of the softmax activation function > 0.5), we retain this data segment as a signal. This criterion differs from the traditional signal-to-noise ratio definition. Ultimately, 128 bursts are preserved.

Data were calibrated using the psrchive package Hotan et al. (2004). On and off-source observations of the standard calibrator 3C 380 were used to measure the intensity of the L-Band receiver’s built-in noise diode. The noise diode was observed again at the position of FRB 20220912A prior to the main observations, and these data were used to flux calibrate each burst from the FRB. Polarization calibration was achieved by correcting for the differential gain and phase between the receptors through separate measurements of a noise diode signal injected at an angle of 45 ◦ from the linear receptors with the single-axis model using the PSRCHIVE software package. To excise radio frequency interference (RFI), we used the PSRCHIVE software package to adopt median filter to each burst in the frequency domain with the command paz and we also mitigated RFI of each burst manually with the command pazi.

entries.name: flux and polarization calibration using psrchive

evidence.original_text: We measured the RM using the methods of Stokes QU-fitting (O’Sullivan et al. 2012). We selected positions where the signal exceeds the noise by 3 sigma for RM fitting. For each burst, we fitted an average RM. We calculated the degrees of linear polarization and circular polarization for bursts with RM detection. We first derotated the linear polarization with the measured RM. The measured linear polarization is overestimated in the presence of noise. Therefore we use the frequency-averaged, de-biased total linear polarization (Everett & Weisberg 2001; Day et al. 2020): where σI is the Stokes I off-pulse standard deviation and Li is the measured frequency-averaged linear polarization of time sample i . We defined the degree of linear polarization as (Σ iL de-bias,i)/(Σ iIi) and that of circular polarization as (Σ iVi)/(Σ iIi), where the summation is over the time samples in one burst and Vi is the measured frequency-averaged circular polarization of time sample i . Defining I = Σ iIi, L = Σ iL de-bias,i and V = Σ iVi, uncertainties on the linear polarization fraction and circular polarization fraction are calculated as: where N is the number of time samples of the burst (signal exceeds the noise by 3 σ), and ρ = L, V for linear and circular polarization fraction, respectively.

We use DM ~~p~~ hase for DM optimization. After the flux calibration, we calculated the peak flux density S peak and fluence F from the data. Due to the presence of RFI in many bursts, we determined the upper and lower cutoff frequencies (bandwidth) where the signal is submerged in noise, through visual inspection. The choice of bandwidth does not affect the energy calculation as long as it adequately covers the burst. In the case of bursts with substructure, we defined the burst bandwidth based on the widest structure within it. The pulse profile was obtained by averaging the flux in burst bandwidth along the frequency. The peak flux is the maximum value of the pulse profile with the time resolution of 655.36 µ s. Specific fluence is the integration of the burst profile with respect to time, and the equivalent width W eq is computed by dividing the specific fluence by the peak flux.

FRB 20220912A was observed between 1.1–1.9 GHz with the GBT’s L-Band receiver and the Versatile Green Bank Astronomical Spectrometer (VEGAS) digital backend (Prestage et al. 2015) on 24 October 2022 starting at 00:30:46 UTC and lasting 2 hours. After setting up and calibration, the on-source time was 1.4 hours. The data were coherently dedispersed at a DM of 219.46 pc cm −3 and recorded in the psrfits standard format (Hotan et al. 2004). Full-Stokes spectra were recorded every 81.92 µ s with 0.195 MHz-wide channels.

entries.setup: GBT L-Band receiver and VEGAS backend, 1.1–1.9 GHz, 1.4 hours on-source

evidence.original_text: For the GBT data, we apply dispersion compensation with DM = 219.46 pc cm −3 , followed by fourfold data subsampling. The data are then divided into distinct time segments, each consisting of 4096 samples (post-subsampling), and reshaped into a size of 512 × 512. These segments are subsequently fed into the model for prediction. When the probability of a signal in the model-predicted data segment exceeds 50% (as the output of the softmax activation function > 0.5), we retain this data segment as a signal. This criterion differs from the traditional signal-to-noise ratio definition. Ultimately, 128 bursts are preserved.

entries.setup: GBT data, DM compensation, DRAFTS model, 128 bursts detected

evidence.original_text: We show the RM of FRB 20220912A in Figure 1. The RMs have been corrected for the effect of the ionosphere. We calculate the ionosphere’s RM by using a python code - `IonFR` (Sotomayor-Beltran et al. 2013) and global ionospheric map (GIM) products. The GIM products are available on NASA’s website in the IONosphere EXchange (IONEX) format. For our GBT observation, the ionosphere contribution is 1.69 ± 0.06 rad m −2 . In Figure 1, we only selected bursts with RM error bars smaller than 5 rad m −2 . We note that burst 89 has a RM of 14.5 ± 0.6 rad m −2 . This RM measurement is an outlier and is caused by frequency-dependant polarization. Excluding this measurement and bursts with RM error bars larger than 5 rad m −2 , our GBT measurements have a mean RM of −0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 .

entries.setup: Stokes QU-fitting, IonFR code with GIM products

evidence.original_text: We detected 128 bursts from FRB 20220912A in 1.4 hours with the GBT (see Methods 2.1 for details of the observation). The time of arrivals, peak flux density, width, fluence, energy, DM, RM and degrees of linear and circular polarization of the bursts are listed in Table 1. The average burst rate is about 90 hr⁻¹, which is the highest for fast radio burst observations at the GBT or any other (sub)-100 meter radio telescopes. The previous highest burst rate at the GBT is 21 hr⁻¹ of FRB 20121102A (Gajjar et al. 2018). Nimmo et al. (2023) reported a burst storm of 53 bursts occuring within only 40 minutes from FRB 20200120E at Effelsberg telescope, which corresponds to a burst rate of 80 hr⁻¹.

entries.context: FRB 20220912A observed with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022, with 1.4 hours on-source time

evidence.original_text: We show the RM of FRB 20220912A in Figure 1. The RMs have been corrected for the effect of the ionosphere. We calculate the ionosphere’s RM by using a python code - `IonFR` (Sotomayor-Beltran et al. 2013) and global ionospheric map (GIM) products. The GIM products are available on NASA’s website in the IONosphere EXchange (IONEX) format. For our GBT observation, the ionosphere contribution is 1.69 ± 0.06 rad m⁻². In Figure 1, we only selected bursts with RM error bars smaller than 5 rad m⁻². We note that burst 89 has a RM of 14.5 ± 0.6 rad m⁻². This RM measurement is an outlier and is caused by frequency-dependant polarization. Excluding this measurement and bursts with RM error bars larger than 5 rad m⁻², our GBT measurements have a mean RM of −0.4 ± 0.3 rad m⁻². We did not observe obvious intraday RM change in FRB 20220912A in contrast to other active repeating FRBs. FRB 20121102A shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m⁻² (Hilmarsson et al. 2021). Similar to FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B also shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m⁻² (AnnaThomas et al. 2023). FRB 20201124A has a smaller intraday RM change of a few tens rad m⁻² (Xu et al. 2022).

entries.context: FRB 20220912A, after correcting for ionosphere contribution (1.69 ± 0.06 rad m⁻²) and excluding outlier burst 89 (RM 14.5 ± 0.6 rad m⁻²) and bursts with RM error > 5 rad m⁻²

evidence.original_text: Out of the 128 detected bursts, 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. For bursts with peak flux density greater than 500 mJy, 56% of the bursts have circular polarization with signal-to-noise ratio > 5. The highest absolute fractional circular polarization reaches 58%. FRB 20220912A is the 4th reported repeating FRB with circular polarization. The previous reported repeating FRBs with circular polarization are all active FRBs, namely FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022), FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a). The fraction of bursts with circular polarization is less than 10% for FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a), much smaller than FRB 20220912A.

entries.context: 128 bursts detected from FRB 20220912A with GBT

evidence.original_text: Out of the 128 detected bursts, 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. For bursts with peak flux density greater than 500 mJy, 56% of the bursts have circular polarization with signal-to-noise ratio > 5. The highest absolute fractional circular polarization reaches 58%. FRB 20220912A is the 4th reported repeating FRB with circular polarization. The previous reported repeating FRBs with circular polarization are all active FRBs, namely FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022), FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et. al. 2022a). The fraction of bursts with circular polarization is less than 10% for FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a), much smaller than FRB 20220912A.

entries.context: bright bursts from FRB 20220912A with peak flux density > 500 mJy and signal-to-noise ratio > 5

evidence.original_text: The average burst rate is about 90 hr⁻¹, which is the highest for fast radio burst observations at the GBT or any other (sub)-100 meter radio telescopes. The previous highest burst rate at the GBT is 21 hr⁻¹ of FRB 20121102A (Gajjar et al. 2018). Nimmo et al. (2023) reported a burst storm of 53 bursts occuring within only 40 minutes from FRB 20200120E at Effelsberg telescope, which corresponds to a burst rate of 80 hr⁻¹.

entries.compared_to: GBT observations of FRB 20121102A, Effelsberg observations of FRB 20200120E

evidence.original_text: We did not observe obvious intraday RM change in FRB 20220912A in contrast to other active repeating FRBs. FRB 20121102A shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m⁻² (Hilmarsson et al. 2021). Similar to FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B also shows intraday RM change of a few hundred rad m⁻² (AnnaThomas et al. 2023). FRB 20201124A has a smaller intraday RM change of a few tens rad m⁻² (Xu et al. 2022). In Figure 1, we also show the RM measurements from CHIME (McKinven & Chime/Frb Collaboration 2022) and NRT (Hewitt et al. 2023). The RM does not change in about a month’s time. The estimated Galactic RM contribution in this line of sight is −15 ± 11 rad m⁻² (Hutschenreuter et al. 2022). The local environment of FRB 20220912A thus contributes little RM. The small and stable RMs indicate a non-magneto-ionic environment of FRB 20220912A.

FRB 20220912A is the 4th reported repeating FRB with circular polarization. The previous reported repeating FRBs with circular polarization are all active FRBs, namely FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022), FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a). The fraction of bursts with circular polarization is less than 10% for FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a), much smaller than FRB 20220912A.

Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are bright radio bursts originating at cosmological distances. Only three repeating FRBs FRB 20121102A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20201124A among ∼ 60 known repeating FRBs have circular polarization. We observed the FRB 20220912A with the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at L-band on 24 October 2022 and detected 128 bursts in 1.4 hours, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest yet for FRBs observed by the GBT. The average rotation measure (RM) was −0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with negligible intraday RM change, indicating a likely non-magneto-ionic environment. 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. Approximately 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. A downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings were found in our sample. The characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely to be caused by the magneto-ionic environment for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

We report the extreme activity of FRB 20220912A and its likely non-magneto-ionic environment. Our main results are the following. 1. 128 bursts were detected in 1.4 hours with GBT, corresponding to a burst rate of about 90 hr −1 , which is the highest for any FRB observed by GBT or any other (sub) 100-m radio telescopes. 2. It has an average RM of -0.4 ± 0.3 rad m −2 with unnoticeable intraday RM change, which indicates that it is in a non-magneto-ionic environment. 3. Out of the 128 detected bursts, 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. 56% of the bright bursts have circular polarization. 4. Some bursts from FRB 20220912A show downward drift in frequency and polarization angle swings. Our results have increased the number of repeating FRBs with circular polarization from three to four. The likely non-magneto-ionic environment of FRB 20220912A and characterization of FRB 20220912A indicates that the circular polarization is unlikely caused by the magneto-ionic environment, but more likely related to the central engine, for at least some of the repeating FRB population.

The observation of FRB 20201124A implies that the RM variation could be intermittent for a certain source, meanwhile, the small mean value might be due to the cancellation of RM at different region. Thus, in the future we will conduct a long-term monitoring campaign for FRB 20220912A, which would reveal whether such a source indeed has a non-magneto-ionic environment. Out of the 128 detected bursts, 61% bursts have linear polarization fraction greater than 90%. For bursts with peak flux density greater than 500 mJy, 56% of the bursts have circular polarization with signal-to-noise ratio > 5. The highest absolute fractional circular polarization reaches 58%. FRB 20220912A is the 4th reported repeating FRB with circular polarization. The previous reported repeating FRBs with circular polarization are all active FRBs, namely FRB 20201124A (Xu et al. 2022), FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a). The fraction of bursts with circular polarization is less than 10% for FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A (Feng et al. 2022a), much smaller than FRB 20220912A.

The observation of FRB 20201124A implies that the RM variation could be intermittent for a certain source, meanwhile, the small mean value might be due to the cancellation of RM at different region. Thus, in the future we will conduct a long-term monitoring campaign for FRB 20220912A, which would reveal whether such a source indeed has a non-magneto-ionic environment.
